sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline addresses ergonomic risk in dynamic field tasks such as inspections, sampling, climbing, kneeling, carrying, and awkward terrain work.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain or fatigue linked to tools, PPE, climbing, kneeling, or uneven-ground work
- Recurrent symptom flare in the same field task pattern
- Difficulty sustaining posture or handling load safely outdoors

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Field ergonomics often involves terrain, weather, access, PPE, and time pressure, not just the tool itself
- A task that is acceptable in clinic simulation may be unsafe in heat, mud, darkness, or confined spaces
- Ergonomic control may require changing sequence or staffing, not just changing posture

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Clarify which field task is provoking symptoms and whether the worker can continue safely today
- Modify exposure quickly when the task is clearly worsening symptoms or safety
- Escalate if field conditions make even reduced duties unsafe

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Review tool use, carry method, reach height, kneeling demands, ladder or stair access, and surface stability
- Recommend practical field controls such as load sharing, route change, task rotation, kneeling support, mechanical aid, or altered PPE setup
- Integrate ergonomic changes with graded rehab and return-to-task progression

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The best field ergonomic fix is usually the one workers can still use in bad weather and under time pressure
